BOOM: @IEA says UAE lifted its crude production to a **record high** of 4.1 million barrels a day in June (including condensates and natural gas liquids, total oil liquids output soared to an impressive 4.8m b/d).

quoted.text:
🚨🚨🚨 We have the first Persian Gulf country lifting its oil exports to (if not even above) pre-war levels: UAE.

Sure, the 'ketchup effect' may be inflating exports a bit (from inventories). But whatever that contribution is, clearly June oil exports have surged to >95% of pre-war levels (and some tanker trackers have UAE June oil exports even above pre-war levels).

With exports surging, the recovery in oil wellhead production won't be far behind, me thinks.

Importantly, UAE oil is flowing well beyond its typical trade partners. With Chinese demand down, Abu Dhabi is shipping lots of crude into Europe and the US.

quoted.text:
The SPR is being consumed as a short-term political and global market stabilizer during a period of active Middle East supply friction, at the cost of long-term readiness and potential cavern integrity. 

With the buffer at 1983 low, the margin for error on further disruptions shrinks dramatically. 

The period from late 2026 into 2027 will reveal how much “bought time” was actually worth the lost resilience, especially once product shortages and food price transmission become unavoidable.

The brutal reality here is most people arent for whats coming. 

 #FoodCrisis #Inflation

#oott

quoted.text:
How do you produce diesel?

1. From the CDU. Each crude will give different gasoil yields and sulphur qualities. Run through the hydro desulphurisation unit to lower sulphur to 10 ppm levels.

2. From the hydrocracker using VGO as feedstock. Yields can vary.

3. From other conversion units (yields and quality tend to be significantly inferior to the hydrocracker).

4. Blend some jet fuel into diesel up to flash point limit

So if refineries are shutting down, which product suffers the most?

#oott

quoted.text:
⚡️ New comments from senior Iranian official to @DropSiteNews on Trump’s recent threats, his claim the Iranians are asking him to talk, and the situation in the Strait of Hormuz ⬇️

🔺A senior Iranian official rejected Trump’s claim that Iran asked the US to continue talks. 

“We have not sent any message to the American side regarding the continuation of peace negotiations,” he said, adding that “diplomatic engagement stems solely from the mediators’ insistence” that the U.S. has expressed a “desire to create a path for Trump to withdraw” from the war. 

🔺Trump continues to flail in his effort to manufacture a victory narrative and Iran has refused to provide him with one. This has continued a cycle of Trump making grand threats to intensify the war and sporadic US bombing of Iran. Trump is now claiming the ceasefire is finished but talks will still continue. 

🔺The Iranian official said that Iran will not tolerate any attempts by the U.S. to subvert Iranian authority over the Strait of Hormuz. 

“In recent days, Trump has issued direct instructions in an attempt to violate the fifth provision of the [MOU] by imposing uncoordinated vessel movements in the Strait of Hormuz, bypassing Iran’s monitoring mechanism,” he said. “From our perspective, the violation of any single provision of the [MOU] constitutes a breach of the agreement as a whole.”

“We will not, under any circumstances, permit the disruption of the established arrangements and monitoring mechanisms in the Strait of Hormuz,“ he added. 

🔺“Our firm determination is to prevent any violation of the terms of the [MOU] by the American side, and any violation will be met with a response.”
quoted.author: jeremyscahill
